cases.rationale: The court rejected the argument, stating it was the plaintiff's own negligence in not having the return amended earlier, not the constable's death, that caused the issue.
In regard to the absence of Frink, it is not shown that it was unavoidable. Neither the act of God, nor any legal process prevented him from being present.
if the principal dies before that time and before surrender, that which it was possible for him to have performed in discharge of his obligation, is made impossible by the act of God.

The law is well settled, where goods are necessarily thrown overboard in a tempest, to preserve the vessel and crew, that it is a loss by inevitable accident, or...the act of God, which excuses the carrier.
The carrier is liable because the baggage was stolen, and 'Loss by theft is not within either of the exceptions [act of God or public enemy] to the risk of a common-carrier.'
A destruction by fire, unless occasioned by lightning, does not fall within the exception. The presumption is that the fire arose from some act of man.
where the party, by his own contract, creates a duty or charge upon himself, he is bound to make it good, notwithstanding any accident or delay by inevitable necessity.
The duty to wait was created by contract, not imposed by law, so the death of a party did not excuse performance as the contract did not provide for such a contingency.

The complainant's failure to secure an attorney to file for an extension was not an 'inevitable accident which ordinary prudence could not have foreseen and avoided, but manifests a want of the most ordinary care.'
Where performance of a personal services contract is prevented by the sickness or death of the party, a recovery may be had for the labor actually done.
a common carrier cannot, by a general notice, even if brought home to the owner of the goods, limit, restrict, or avoid the liability imposed on him by the common law.
The non-performance was not caused by the act of God, nor of the law, nor of the obligee. It was attributable solely to the default of the judge, resulting from acts within his own control.
When a party engages unconditionally by express contract to do an act, performance is not excused by inevitable accident or other unforeseen contingency not within his control.
Even if the loss of the vessel was an act of God, the carrier had a duty to exercise diligence in providing another vessel to substantially perform the contract of carriage.
The squall that sank the sloop was the remote cause. The immediate cause was the defendant's vessel hitting the mast, which does not exclude human agency and is not an act of God.

cases.name_abbreviation: Michaels v. New York Central Railroad
cases.rationale: If the act or negligence of man contributes to bring or leave the goods of the carrier under the operation of natural causes that work their injury, he is not excused.
The carrier cannot avail himself of the exception to his liability which the law has created, unless he has been free from negligence or fault himself.
The court affirmed judgment against the innkeeper because the loss of the guest's property was not traced to an act of God or any other recognized exception to the rule of strict liability.
It is his own voluntary act which has placed him under the so-called vis major, and therefore the phrase, as to him, is a solecism.
The legislature cannot have intended to include those cases in which, by the act of God, a literal obedience to their mandate has become impossible.
The plaintiff had not undertaken to erect and finish this building... Nor was it possible, after its prostration, for him to perform any part of his work until the walls were replaced.
The inability of the principal to appear by reason of sickness is, within these cases, the act of God, which excuses, and is a sufficient defence.
The contractor's undertaking embraced all but the acts of God or the public enemy; the court reversed and remanded for a new trial with proper instructions on liability.
The delivery of that portion was, under such circumstances, rendered impossible by the act of God.

cases.name_abbreviation: Austin v. New Jersey Steamboat Co.
cases.rationale: The phrase 'act of God' is not used, but the court rejects the synonymous 'inevitable accident' defense, as the defendant's own negligence put them in a position where the accident occurred.
A condition is implied that the impossibility of performance arising from the perishing of the thing shall excuse the performance, regardless of whether it was an inevitable accident or act of God.

Where a party, by his own contract, creates a duty or charge upon himself, he is bound to make it good... notwithstanding any accident by inevitable necessity, because he might have provided against it.
Payment was a condition precedent to the continuance of the policy, and no mere accident or act of God, however controlling, could continue the policy in force after the pay day without payment.
Detention by the close of navigation, which is the act of God... does not have the effect to terminate or dissolve a contract of affreightment.

cases.name_abbreviation: First National Bank v. President & Directors of Insurance Co. of North America
cases.rationale: The court held the impossibility defense failed because it was the assured's own default (not paying a judgment) that caused the sheriff's levy, which prevented performance.
The plaintiff's argument that the arbitrator misapplied the 'inevitable accident' exception failed, as the court found the arbitrator did not rely on that clause at all.
If their possession was as carriers they are liable, as the fire was not the result of the act of God.

cases.name_abbreviation: Chicago & Northwestern Railway Co. v. Dickinson
cases.rationale: The railroad's liability was based on its failure to deliver the corn on time, before the fire began. "In this case there was no fire in existence at the time the corn should have been delivered."

cases.name_abbreviation: Merchants' Despatch Transportation Co. v. Kahn
cases.rationale: Had they shipped the goods by the way of Indianapolis, as they had previously shipped other goods to these parties, the loss would not have occurred.
The court held the fire was not an 'act of God,' which it defined as 'something superhuman, or something in opposition to the act of man.'

cases.name_abbreviation: American Merchants' Union Express Co. v. Wolf
cases.rationale: In this case, the goods were not delivered to the consignee, nor was the company prevented by the act of God or a public enemy.

cases.name_abbreviation: Evans v. United States Life Insurance
cases.rationale: The court found the facts did not sustain the claim of impossibility, and that the insured 'took the chances of being able to return' when he traveled south knowing the policy terms.

cases.name_abbreviation: Merchants' Despatch Transportation Co. v. Theilbar
cases.rationale: We held...that the same fire by which these goods were destroyed could not be regarded as the act of God, so as to exempt common carriers, on that ground, from their common law liability.

cases.name_abbreviation: Ansonia Brass & Copper Co. v. Babbitt
cases.rationale: The seizure of property by a marshal was 'in no proper sense a yielding to a vis major' as the sheriff had legal remedies to protect his possession.

cases.name_abbreviation: Chicago & Northwestern Railway Co. v. Hoag
cases.rationale: To claim exemption for an act of nature, it must be unforeseeable. Appellant must be held to have known the water would freeze, it being a fact occurring in the course of nature.

cases.name_abbreviation: Wheeler v. Connecticut Mutual Life Insurance
cases.rationale: The insured's insanity did not excuse non-payment of the premium because the act could have been performed by another person on his behalf; performance was not impossible.

cases.name_abbreviation: People v. Globe Mutual Life Insurance
cases.rationale: The party claiming breach failed to show the company was at fault for the state-ordered dissolution, which could have resulted from blameless external forces ('vis major').
The court accepts that a sheriff should not be liable when 'entirely without fault, and has by vis major been disabled... from executing its command.'

cases.name_abbreviation: Tarbell v. Royal Exchange Shipping Co.
cases.rationale: The court found the consignees were not prevented from removing the goods by any vis major, and that removal was practicable, thus terminating the carrier's stringent liability.
Between the acts of the defendants and the injury, there was not an interposition of... any extraordinary natural phenomena, to break the causal connection.
The court, having been regularly opened, did not lose jurisdiction by failing to appear on the adjourned day due to an extraordinary snow storm that made it impossible for judges to appear.
The contract was construed to allow for 'valid reasons' for non-payment, which the court found was intended to include being prevented by an act of God, thus making the forfeiture conditional.

cases.name_abbreviation: Ohio & Mississippi Railway Co. v. Ramey
cases.rationale: The duty is to provide against not just ordinary floods, but extraordinary floods that should have been anticipated because they have occasionally occurred in the past.

cases.name_abbreviation: Ward v. Hudson River Building Co.
cases.rationale: Having contracted absolutely to complete the houses on or before a certain date, unforeseen contingencies, no matter of what nature, are not available to plaintiff as a defense.

cases.name_abbreviation: People ex rel. Mitchell v. Martin
cases.rationale: The statute...will lead to no such result as to deprive an honorable police officer of his place on the force by reason of absence arising from the act of God.

cases.name_abbreviation: Wald v. Pittsburg, Cincinnati, Chicago & St. Louis R. R. Co.
cases.rationale: The act of God defense fails if the carrier's negligence was an active and co-operative cause of the loss; whether the carrier was negligent in separating the baggage was a question for the jury.
The referee found 'that by the act of Gfod, as well as by the perils of the sea,' ... the lumber was lost overboard, and without the fault or neglect of the plaintiffs...

cases.name_abbreviation: Standard Brewery v. Bemis & Curtis Malting Co.
cases.rationale: The court held the contract was a bailment for hire requiring only ordinary care, so the bailee was not liable for the goods perishing in a fire without his fault.

cases.name_abbreviation: Rock Island & Peoria Railway Co. v. Krapp
cases.rationale: if the same injury and damage, must have happened, in any event, in the state existing before the act complained of, defendant would not be liable to him.
The law allows a party to extinguish an easement after its destruction by an act of God, but here the defendants revived it by rebuilding the property in the same manner as before the fire.

cases.name_abbreviation: Buffalo & Lancaster Land Co. v. Bellevue Land & Improvement Co.
cases.rationale: The trial court found for the plaintiff despite also finding that a winter of 'unusual severity' made performance 'practically impossible' for the defendant.
It was not indispensable to recovery that appellee should have shown the cause or force which put the barrel in motion... was not that of nature, as a wind, etc.

cases.name_abbreviation: Elgin, Aurora & Southern Traction Co. v. Wilson
cases.rationale: The carrier was not relieved by a stranger's tortious act because its own failure to use foresight (e.g., by not locking the switch) presented the opportunity for the act to occur.
The executor of a life tenant is entitled to crops because the estate was of uncertain duration and 'determined by the act of God.' This is the doctrine of emblements.
The defendant argued the wind was not foreseeable, but the court credited evidence that the foreman was warned that 'it looked like a storm was coming up' and had time to secure the traveler.

cases.name_abbreviation: Cormack v. New York & Hartford Railroad
cases.rationale: The snow storm which obstructed the defendant’s train in the present case was clearly an act of God within the meaning of that phrase in the rule of law.

cases.name_abbreviation: Whiteside v. North American Accident Insurance
cases.rationale: ...when a person by express contract engages absolutely to do an act not impossible or unlawful at the time, neither inevitable accident, nor other unforeseen contingency... will excuse him...

cases.name_abbreviation: Barnet v. New York Central & Hudson River Railroad
cases.rationale: The loss was caused by an extraordinary flood, an act of God, and there was no affirmative evidence on which the jury might base a finding of negligence which directly contributed to the loss.
The court affirmed a verdict for the defendants, as the issue of negligence was controverted on the grounds that the storms were such as they were not bound to anticipate and provide against.
Ella Knapp has not provided a home for Frank Shoemaker 'only because his death rendered it impossible.'
The closing of a school by the order of a school board or a board of health is not the act of God, however prudent and necessary it may have been to make such order.

cases.name_abbreviation: Abell Chevrolet Co. v. Industrial Commission
cases.rationale: Before an injury from an act of God may be said to have arisen from the employment, it must be shown there was a special or greater risk to the employee...than to the general public.

cases.name_abbreviation: Abell Chevrolet Co. v. Industrial Commission
cases.rationale: We cannot say from the record that there was an increased hazard to defendant in error, arising out of his employment, which subjected him to an exceptional risk greater than to other persons.

cases.name_abbreviation: Blue v. St. Clair Country Club
cases.rationale: The defendant's own witnesses testified that there was nothing unusual about this particular whirlwind... Such conditions of nature, which experience shows are likely to occur, should also have been reasonably foreseen.

cases.name_abbreviation: Inland Steel Co. v. Industrial Commission
cases.rationale: The rule is that before an injury from an act of God may be said to have arisen from the employment it must be shown there was a special or greater risk to the employee... than to the general public.

cases.name_abbreviation: Campbell "66" Express, Inc. v. Industrial Commission
cases.rationale: The Commission could reasonably conclude that the claimant was subjected to risks of injury from this tornado beyond that to which the general public was exposed.
The trial court, granting summary judgment, stated it would be 'against public policy to hold one liable for acts of God' where damages were the 'direct result of inclement weather.'
